title: Authorization in Keycloak
description:
## Two Ways to Authorize

Authentication answers *who are you?*; **authorization** answers *what are you allowed to do?*. Keycloak offers two layers, and most applications start simple and only adopt the second when they truly need fine-grained control.

### 1. Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)

The lightweight, common approach. You define **realm roles** and **client roles**, assign them (directly, via groups, or through composite roles), and Keycloak embeds them in the issued tokens (typically under `realm_access.roles` and `resource_access.<client>.roles`). **The application itself enforces** access by inspecting those claims:

```text
GET /reports   →  app reads token  →  has "reports-viewer" role? → 200 : 403
```

Keycloak only *carries* the roles; your code (or a framework/gateway) makes the decision.

### 2. Authorization Services (fine-grained)

When role checks scattered across your code become unmanageable — or when decisions depend on *attributes*, *context*, or *complex rules* — a confidential client can be turned into a **resource server** and **delegate decisions to Keycloak**. You model the domain centrally:

| Building block | What it is |
|---|---|
| **Resource** | A protected thing (e.g. `Invoice`, `/api/reports`, a document). Can have a type, URI, owner. |
| **Scope** | An action/aspect on a resource (e.g. `view`, `edit`, `delete`). |
| **Policy** | A reusable rule that evaluates to *grant* or *deny*. Types include **role**, **user**, **group**, **time**, **client**, **regex**, **JavaScript**, and **aggregate** (combine other policies with AND/OR). |
| **Permission** | Ties **resources (+ scopes)** to one or more **policies**. "To `edit` an `Invoice`, the *manager-policy* must pass." |

The app asks Keycloak for a decision instead of hardcoding `if role == ...`. This happens through the **token endpoint** using the UMA grant (request a **Requesting Party Token / RPT**), or transparently via the **Policy Enforcer** adapter that sits in front of your endpoints.

### Evaluation flow

> Request → (Policy Enforcer or RPT request) → Keycloak matches the **resource + scope**, finds the applicable **permissions**, evaluates each linked **policy** → combined result → **grant / deny**.

### Which should I use?

| Need | Use |
|---|---|
| A handful of coarse roles, app enforces | **RBAC** (realm/client roles in the token) |
| Centralized, fine-grained, attribute/context-aware decisions managed outside code | **Authorization Services** |

Start with RBAC. Reach for Authorization Services when policies must change without redeploying the app, or when access depends on more than "has role X".
